Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of the British Quantity Surveying method for taking off measurements and Bills of Quantities:

## What is a Bill of Quantities?

A bill of quantities (BoQ) is a document generally prepared by a cost consultant (such as a quantity surveyor) that provides project-specific measured quantities of the items identified in drawings and specifications for a built asset such as a building. A bill of quantities is a document used in tendering in the construction industry in which materials, parts, and labor (and their costs) are itemized. It also (ideally) details the terms and conditions of the construction or repair contract and itemizes all work to enable a contractor to price the work for which he or she is bidding.

## The "Taking Off" Process

The term 'taking off' refers to the process of identifying elements of construction works that can be measured and priced. Taking Off actually is measurement of dimensions, and the compilation of descriptions from the drawing and specification. Those elements can be measured in number, length, area, volume, weight or time, then collated and structured to produce an un-priced bill of quantities.

## Standard Measurement Methods

In the UK, bills of quantities for general construction works were until recently most commonly prepared in accordance with the Standard Method of Measurement 7th Edition (SMM7). However, the New Rules of Measurement (NRM) became operative on 1 January 2013 and replaced SMM7 on 1st July 2013.

## Benefits and Purpose

The bill of quantities assists tenderers in the calculation of construction costs for their tender, and, as it means all tendering contractors will be pricing the same quantities (rather than taking-off quantities from the drawings and specifications themselves), it also provides a fair and accurate system for tendering. Bill of quantities are prepared by quantity surveyors and building estimators, and "Indeed the bill of quantities was the raison d'Ãªtre for the development of quantity surveying as a separate profession."

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about NRM1, NRM2, and SMM7 measurement standards in RICS quantity surveying:

## Overview of the Standards

The New Rules of Measurement (NRM) are published by the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS) and provide a standard set of measurement rules for estimating, cost planning, procurement and whole-life costing for construction projects.

NRM comprises three volumes: NRM1 (Order of cost estimating and cost planning for capital building works), NRM2 (Detailed measurement for building works, replacing SMM7), and NRM3 (Order of cost estimating and cost planning for building maintenance works).

## SMM7 Background

SMM7 was published in 1988. In 2006, SMM7 was due to be updated to SMM8, but following an investigation by a RICS quantity surveying and construction professional group into current practices, it was decided that new standards were required to encourage best practice and reflect changes in construction processes and working practices.

## Key Differences

NRM2 directly replaces SMM7 and is used for pricing Bills of Quantities and consists of detailed breakdowns using unit rates.

Main changes include: element-based structure instead of trade-based, only defined provisional sums, more detailed preliminaries rules, and more items measured separately rather than deemed included. For example, NRM2 measures reveals, sills, and copings separately where SMM7 deemed them included.

This is the first time that the RICS has given guidance on coding and work breakdown structures in an attempt to align the bills with the cost plans produced using NRM1.

## Implementation Timeline

NRM2 became operative on 1 January 2013 and replaced SMM7 on 1 July 2013. NRM2 is recommended by RICS as best practice, though SMM7 is still being used successfully within the UK and around the world.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Bills of Quantities format, structure, and line items in UK construction:

## Definition and Purpose

A Bill of Quantities (BoQ) is a precise list of all the materials, labour, and construction equipment needed for a project, with quantities and descriptions used for cost estimation and overall project management. A BoQ is prepared by the employer's cost consultant and sets out measured quantities of all the items of work identified by the drawings and specifications in the tender documentation.

## Standard Format and Structure

The estimator groups each item under the relevant work section and codes it using the NRM or SMM7 rules, assigns units of measure â€“ deciding whether each line is priced per metre, square metre, cubic metre, hour, day, or lump sum.

Measured items are grouped under appropriate headings (preliminaries, substructure, superstructure, services, finishes, etc.)

## Line Item Components

The four key components of a standard BoQ are: Item descriptions: Clear, specific descriptions of each component or task needed; Quantities: Exact amounts or measurements of each item, e.g. m2, m3, etc; Unit rates: The cost per unit of each item, based on historical data or current market prices.

For each line item, it specifies the description of works, unit, specification, quantity, unit cost, total cost, and community contribution.

## Types of BoQ Structures

Depending on the project, BOQs can be structured in different ways: Elemental BOQ: Grouped by building components (e.g., substructure, finishes); Trade-Based BOQ: Organized by trades (e.g., mechanical, electrical).

A typical structure includes: Preliminaries: site setup, temporary works, welfare, and insurance; Measured works: broken down by trade, excavation, brickwork, joinery, plastering, plumbing, and electrical; Provisional sums: allowances for uncertain works (e.g., ground conditions).

## Key Approaches and Techniques

By training a Deep Learning algorithm, the model can better generalize the elements in different types of images, improving the performance in identifying desired elements.

### Deep Learning Architectures

Researchers use modified FCN networks with Resnet50 backbone for line segment detection, and YOLOv4 for number recognition in floor plan drawings. Comparative studies examine deep learning-based object detection architectures including Tesseract, EAST, CRAFT, Faster R-CNN, YOLOv5, YOLOR, YOLOv7, and YOLOv8.

### Recent Innovations

Vision Transformer (ViT) applies the Transformer architecture to images by breaking them into "patches" and using self-attention mechanisms to analyze global dependencies between patches. Building layouts are problems of global arrangement where relationships between distant rooms matter, making ViT excel at modeling the overall layout.

### Recognition Challenges

Floor plan recognition is more complex than plain text recognition as it involves special symbols and abbreviations that present a challenge for ready-made solutions. Traditional OCR systems are inadequate for architectural drawings and cannot handle the diversity of fonts, text orientations, and overlaps with graphical elements common in plans.

### Applications

Computer Vision uses deep learning models for object detection to identify and place bounding boxes around discrete elements like doors, windows, plumbing fixtures, and electrical outlets. Companies like Measure Square use floor plan analysis technology based on Deep Learning and Computer Vision to detect doors, windows, and rooms from images or documents.

Based on the search results, here are several publicly available floor plan datasets and construction drawing resources for machine learning:

## Major CAD Drawing Datasets

**FloorPlanCAD** contains over 15,000 floor plans, ranging from residential to commercial buildings, and CAD drawings in the dataset are in the SVG format. The project was shut down as of January 2023, though the dataset remains accessible.

**ArchCAD-400K** comprises 5,538 complete drawings and 413,062 chunks, surpassing FloorPlanCAD by over 26 times. It includes 27 semantic categories such as structural components, non-structural elements, and drawing notations, with 14 categories each containing over 1 million primitives.

## Specialized Floor Plan Datasets

**Modified Swiss Dwellings (MSD)** is the first large-scale floor plan dataset that contains a significant share of layouts of multi-apartment dwellings.

**MLSTRUCT-FP** can be downloaded by requesting a public download link, with each file representing a distinct floor whose labels (wall polygons, slabs) and metadata are stored within a JSON file.

**ResPlan** is a dataset of 17,000 residential floor plans released under a permissive open-source license for public use as a community resource.

## General Purpose Datasets

The **Floor Plans 500** dataset is an open-source collection that provides labeled data to train object detection models to identify doors, windows, and functional zones.

These datasets support various machine learning applications including symbol detection, floor plan generation, and architectural analysis.

## Overview

AI for reading construction drawings uses computer vision and machine learning to understand the content of architectural, structural, and MEP drawings. These systems can identify building elements like walls, doors, and windows, read text and dimensions, understand symbols, and extract information.

## Core Capabilities

Through advanced computer vision and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) technologies, AI systems can automatically detect and label critical elements in technical drawings, such as walls, doors, windows, rooms, dimensions, and materials. AI technology can rapidly interpret complex drawings, extract precise measurements, and identify specific elements like structural components, electrical systems, and plumbing layouts.

## Measurement Extraction Challenges

Extracting dimensions accurately from engineering drawings remains a significant challenge, especially at scale. Manual extraction is labor-intensive, error-prone, and difficult to integrate into digital workflows. Automating this task with AI could streamline production pipelines, reduce human oversight, and enable powerful applications such as automated design validation and real-time manufacturing feedback.

## Advanced Approaches

By leveraging each class of models for their strengths and using them in a complementary fashion to address their weaknesses, researchers achieved 97% accuracy on determining whether each attribute of a plan was compliant with building codes compared with only ~60% accuracy with the Naive approach. An integrated approach achieves 96.8% overall accuracy across all information extracted from plans by combining precise element extraction from small, task-specific models with the textual understanding and reasoning ability of large foundation models.

Based on the web search results, I found highly relevant information about your query. The search revealed a research paper that directly addresses vision-language model fine-tuning for construction drawings:

## Key Finding: Florence-2 Fine-tuning for Engineering Drawings

A study proposes an automated GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing) extraction method by fine-tuning Florence-2, an open-source vision-language model. The fine-tuning approach uses an open-source VLM model trained on a dataset of 400 drawings with ground truth annotations provided by domain experts.

### Model Comparison

The study evaluates two state-of-the-art closed-source VLMs, GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet, on the same dataset. Due to the computational cost and impracticality of fine-tuning large closed-source VLMs for domain-specific tasks, GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet are evaluated in a zero-shot setting, while Florence-2, a smaller model with 0.23 billion parameters, is optimized through full-parameter fine-tuning.

### Performance Results

Florence-2 achieves a 29.95% increase in precision, a 37.75% increase in recall, a 52.40% improvement in F1-score, and a 43.15% reduction in hallucination rate compared to the best-performing closed-source model.

This research demonstrates that fine-tuning smaller, open-source models like Florence-2 can be more effective than relying on large closed-source models for domain-specific construction drawing analysis tasks.

**Challenges and Limitations:**
While AI greatly reduces manual errors, risks may still arise if source drawings are unclear, incomplete, or inconsistently formatted, and overreliance without human verification can lead to blind spots, especially in complex or non-standardized designs. Donut is an OCR-free end-to-end Transformer model for document understanding that doesn't require off-the-shelf OCR engines/APIs and shows state-of-the-art performance on visual document understanding tasks including information extraction. ## Key Challenges in Quantity Surveying Takeoffs

**Scale and Measurement Issues:**

Errors in scaling, incorrect dimensions, or outdated drawings result from rushing through measurements, and inaccurate data can lead to material delays or insufficient quantities, resulting in costly rework, schedule delays, and budget overruns.

**Plan Reading and Dimension Annotation:**
Blueprints include dimensions that indicate the size and placement of various elements, and notes may provide further details about materials, finishes, and construction methodsâ€”details that are critical for accurate quantity takeoffs and cost estimation. Errors and discrepancies can be quickly found if the dimension sheets are annotated and the takeoff is laid out clearly and logically.

**Spatial Visualization:**
The principal challenge quantity surveyors encounter is taking two-dimension plans, converting them into three-dimensional images, and coming up with takeoffs.

**Digital Scale Detection:**
When you upload a sheet, the system analyses title blocks, scale bars, and dimension annotations to suggest the correct scale instantly. However, even though AI scale detection is highly accurate, always perform a quick sanity check by measuring one or two known dimensions to ensure the detected scale matches your expectations before starting the takeoff.

CubiCasa5K is a large-scale floorplan image dataset containing 5000 samples annotated into over 80 floorplan object categories.

The dataset annotations are performed in a dense and versatile manner by using polygons for separating the different objects. Rooms include 12 baseline classes: Background, Outdoor, Wall, Kitchen, Living Room, Bedroom, Bath, Hallway, Railing, Storage, Garage, Other Rooms; Icon/opening includes 11 baseline classes: Window, Door, Closet, Electrical Appliance, Toilet, Sink, Sauna Bench, Fire Place, Bathtub, Chimney, Empty.

### Annotation Pipeline

The annotation pipeline employs a structured protocol: wall polygons are drawn first, followed by room segmentation (using wall boundaries), and finally icon/opening placement.

# Revit Family Engine

AI-powered Revit family creation system using a locally fine-tuned LLM. Generates
compilable C# Revit API code from natural language descriptions of parametric family
geometry, parameters, constraints, GD&T annotations, and Dynamo scripts.

## Architecture

Three-tier system, all running locally:

1. **Revit Add-in** (`addin/`) -- C#/.NET 8.0 add-in with ribbon commands
2. **FastAPI Backend** (`backend/`, port 8001) -- LLM routing and parameter resolution
3. **Ollama LLM** -- Fine-tuned Qwen2.5-Coder-32B via QLoRA

Integrates with the `dynamo-node-search` Dynamo specialist and `building-code-mcp`
compliance server in the same ecosystem.

## Seven Domains

| Domain | Coverage |
|---|---|
| `family_geometry` | Extrusion, Revolution, Blend, Sweep, void forms |
| `family_parameters` | FamilyManager, FamilyParameter, formulas, type catalogs |
| `constraints` | ReferencePlane, dimensions, FamilyLabel, EqualConstraint |
| `family_types` | Type iteration, nested family loading, CSV type catalogs |
| `gdt` | ASME Y14.5-2018 tolerances via Revit DimensionType |
| `dynamo` | Python Dynamo node scripts (ProtoGeometry / RevitNodes) |
| `building_code` | IBC/NFPA compliance validation |

## Quick Start

```bash
# Backend
python -m venv venv && venv/Scripts/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
uvicorn backend.main:app --reload --port 8001

# Generate training data
python training_pipeline/run_pipeline.py --output-dir output

# Fine-tune (requires GPU with ~24GB VRAM for 32B model)
axolotl train axolotl_revit_config.yml

# Deploy to Ollama
ollama create revit-family-32b --modelfile Modelfile
```

## Critical Notes

- Revit internal units: **feet** for length (mm / 304.8), **radians** for angles
- `FamilyManager` operations must occur **outside** `Transaction` blocks
- Geometry creation (`NewExtrusion`, etc.) must occur **inside** `Transaction` blocks
- Add-in runs on port 8001 (not 8000, to avoid conflict with sw-semantic-engine)

## Training Data

The parameterization framework generates training data via Cartesian product:
five predefined parameter spaces produce hundreds of C# code examples automatically.
Three specialized generators (geometry, parameters, GD&T) contribute ~750 additional pairs.

## Related Projects

- `sw-semantic-engine` -- same architecture for SolidWorks COM API
- `dynamo-node-search` -- Dynamo specialist LLM
- `auto-claw` -- automated family creation from manufacturer spec sheets
- `building-code-mcp` -- building code compliance MCP server

## NRM2 Concrete Works Measurement Units

Concrete is measured by volume in mÂ³ (cubic meters), while formwork is measured by area in mÂ².

## Example for Concrete Elements

NRM2 covers in-situ concrete works for foundations, slabs, beams, and columns. Here's how measurement works for these components:

**Measurement Breakdown:**
- Concrete is measured in volume (mÂ³), reinforcement in weight (kg or Tonnes), and formwork in area (mÂ²)

**Practical Example:**
The rules require measuring each component of the concrete foundation individually. For a strip foundation example:
- Measure the foundation's length, width, and depth to calculate the concrete volume
- Measure the area of formwork required for the exposed sides of the concrete

NRM2 includes items like curing, vibrating, or placing concrete by default unless the guidelines explicitly state otherwise.

The concrete division (Work Section 11) in NRM2 provides standardized rules that ensure consistent measurement and description across walls, floors, columns, and other concrete elements used in building works.

content.text:
Now I have comprehensive research. Let me compile the full feasibility assessment.

---

# Feasibility Assessment: Training Data for a British Quantity Surveying Vision Model

---

## Task Reiteration

You asked for a thorough research report -- no code -- covering what would be needed to train a vision model on the British Quantity Surveying method. The eight questions span domain knowledge (what QS is), measurement standards, visual inputs, text outputs, training pair structure, public data availability, candidate base models, and key technical challenges. This is a feasibility assessment only.

---

## 1. What is British Quantity Surveying?

Quantity Surveying (QS) is a construction industry profession whose core purpose is the financial management and cost control of built assets. The discipline originated in Britain and became institutionalised through the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS). Its defining raison d'etre was, and remains, the production of the Bill of Quantities (BoQ).

### Core tasks

**Taking Off**
The foundational task. A quantity surveyor reads architectural and engineering drawings and "takes off" (extracts) every measurable element of work from those drawings. Dimensions are recorded onto dimension paper (a standardised four-column sheet: timesing, dimensions, squaring, description). Elements are measured in standardised units: number (nr), linear metre (m), square metre (m2), cubic metre (m3), kilogram (kg), tonne (t), or hour. The discipline is explicit: taking off is a geometric reading task applied to two-dimensional drawings to produce three-dimensional or linear quantities, then associated with precise NRM-compliant descriptions.

**Measurement and Abstraction Rules**
Not everything visible on a drawing maps to a billable item in an obvious way. NRM2 specifies what is measured, what is "deemed included" (covered by another item's rate), what requires a separate item, and what is given as a Provisional Sum. A QS must know these rules cold. For example, concrete is measured by volume (m3), formwork separately by contact area (m2), and reinforcement separately by weight (kg or tonne). Finishes on beams, columns, and soffits each have distinct rules.

**Bills of Quantities**
The BoQ is the structured output document. It lists every measured item with: a description (written to NRM2 standards), a unit of measure, a quantity, and (in a priced BoQ) a unit rate and total cost. The BoQ serves as the tender document: all contractors price the same quantities, enabling fair comparison. A typical BoQ for a medium commercial building runs to several hundred line items grouped into work sections.

---

## 2. Measurement Standards: NRM1, NRM2, SMM7

### SMM7 (Standard Method of Measurement, 7th Edition)
Published in 1988 by RICS and the BEC. This was the dominant UK standard for approximately 25 years. SMM7 was trade-based (masonry, concrete, joinery, etc.) rather than element-based. Many items were "deemed included" -- that is, subsidiary work was assumed covered by the rate for the primary item. SMM7 is still in use internationally and on legacy projects but has been superseded in the UK.

### NRM Suite (New Rules of Measurement)
Published by RICS, operative from 1 January 2013, replacing SMM7 entirely from 1 July 2013. Three volumes:

- **NRM1** -- Order of Cost Estimating and Cost Planning for Capital Building Works. Used at early design stages (RIBA Stage 1-3) to produce elemental cost plans. Measurement is approximate and element-based (substructure, superstructure, finishes, services, etc.).
- **NRM2** -- Detailed Measurement for Building Works. This is the workhorse. Used at RIBA Stage 4+ to produce Bills of Quantities for tender. Defines 41 work sections with precise measurement rules. This is the primary standard any QS vision model would need to implement.
- **NRM3** -- Order of Cost Estimating and Cost Planning for Building Maintenance Works. Used for lifecycle costing, less relevant to a measurement model.

### Key NRM2 structural differences from SMM7
- Element-based structure replaces trade-based structure, aligning BoQ items to cost plans produced under NRM1.
- Items previously "deemed included" in SMM7 are now measured separately (reveals, sills, copings, etc.).
- Only "defined" provisional sums are allowed (SMM7 allowed "undefined" ones).
- Guidance on work breakdown structure (WBS) coding for the first time.
- More detailed Preliminaries rules.

### NRM2 Work Sections (41 total, abridged list)
1. Preliminaries, 2. Off-site manufactured materials, 3. Demolitions, 4. Alterations/repairs/conservation, 5. Excavating and filling, 6. Ground remediation, 7. Piling, 8. Underpinning, 9. Diaphragm walls, 10. Crib walls, 11. In-situ concrete, 12. Post-tensioned concrete, 13. Precast concrete, 14. Masonry, 15. Structural metalwork, 16. Carpentry, 17. Sheet roof coverings, 18. Tile/slate, 19. Waterproofing, 20. Proprietary linings, 21. Cladding/features, 22. General joinery, 23. Windows, 24. Doors, 25. Stairs/walkways/balustrades, 26. Metalwork, 27. Glazing, 28. Floor finishes, 29. Wall/ceiling finishes, 30. Decoration, 31. Suspended ceilings, 32. Insulation, 33. Furniture/equipment, 34. Mechanical services, 35. Electrical services, 36. Lift/escalator, 37. Fire suppression, 38. Builders' work, 39. Drainage, 40. External works, 41. Minor building works.

### PDF Availability of NRM Documents
NRM2 is publicly accessible via the RICS website in PDF form -- both the 1st edition and 2022 update are linkable from rics.org. They are copyright-protected but downloadable. This is important for training data construction: the measurement rules themselves can be ingested.

---

## 3. Visual Inputs a Vision Model Would Need to Process

A QS working from drawings encounters several drawing types. Each presents different visual parsing challenges.

### Floor Plans (primary)
The most measurement-rich drawing type. Walls, partitions, openings (doors, windows), rooms, dimensions, area annotations, hatching patterns indicating materials, grid lines, and scale bars. Most taking-off work originates here. Plans are typically at 1:50, 1:100, or 1:200 scale. They carry the geometry for walls (m2 or m run), floors (m2), partitions, doors (nr), windows (nr), and room finishes.

### Sections
Cross-sections through the building. Essential for vertical dimensions: floor-to-floor height, slab thickness, roof pitch, foundation depth, retaining wall height. Without sections, many m3 calculations (concrete, excavation) are impossible. Sections also reveal roof construction, parapet details, and basement conditions.

### Elevations
External face views. Used for cladding areas (m2), window and door counts and sizes, external finishes, and parapet/coping lengths. Less dimensioned than plans but essential for external envelope measurement.

### Structural Drawings
Beam and column layouts, slab thicknesses, pad/strip/raft foundation plans. Critical for concrete (m3), formwork (m2), and reinforcement (kg). Structural drawings typically use heavier line weights and reinforcement schedules.

### Roof Plans
Pitched or flat roof areas, drainage outlet positions, rooflights, upstands. Used for roof covering (m2), insulation (m2), drainage (nr/m run).

### Services Drawings (MEP)
Mechanical, electrical, and plumbing layouts. Less central to a first-pass QS vision model but required for NRM2 sections 34-35.

### Schedules and Specifications
Door schedules, window schedules, finishes schedules. These are tabular documents, not graphic drawings. They specify type, size, and finish for every opening or room. A vision model must handle both schematic/graphic inputs and tabular text-heavy inputs.

### Title Blocks
Every drawing sheet carries a title block: project name, drawing number, revision, scale, date. The model must reliably read title blocks to determine scale before any measurement can begin.

---

## 4. Text Outputs the Model Would Produce

### BoQ Line Item Structure (NRM2 compliant)

A fully compliant NRM2 line item contains:

```
Work Section Number | Item Code | Description | Unit | Quantity | Rate | Amount
```

Example outputs:

```
11.1.1  In-situ concrete; mix C25/30; walls; thickness 
        200mm; height 3.0-4.5m                           m3    47.20
        
11.2.1  Formwork; walls; plain finish; height 3.0-4.5m   m2   236.00

14.1.1  Brick walling; Class B engineering bricks; 
        cement mortar (1:3); walls; thickness 215mm       m2   182.40

5.1.1   Excavating; bulk; maximum depth 1.0-2.0m         m3   312.00
```

### Measurement Descriptions (NRM2 compliant language)
NRM2 prescribes how descriptions must be constructed. They follow a hierarchical pattern:
1. Material/work type
2. Nature or quality of material (spec, grade)
3. Support condition or location
4. Dimensioned cross-section or thickness
5. Any supplementary descriptor required by the rules

The model must generate descriptions that conform to this linguistic template, not free-form prose.

### Dimension Paper / Measurement Workings
Intermediate output (optional but valuable for training verification): the four-column dimension paper format showing timesing column (multipliers), dimension columns (L x W x H or L x W, etc.), squaring column (computed quantity), and description. This is the working that precedes the BoQ.

### Structured JSON or CSV Output
For a machine-learning system, the practical output format for training and inference would be structured data:
```json
{
  "work_section": "11",
  "item_code": "11.1.1",
  "description": "In-situ concrete; mix C25/30; walls; thickness 200mm; height 3.0-4.5m",
  "unit": "m3",
  "quantity": 47.20
}
```

---

## 5. What Training Pairs Would Look Like

This is the crux of the feasibility question. There are at least three plausible pairing strategies, in order of increasing complexity.

### Pair Type A: Image Region + Single Measurement Item
**Input**: A cropped region of a floor plan (e.g., a single wall run with its dimension annotation).
**Output**: A single BoQ line item (description + unit + quantity).

This is the simplest framing. It reduces the problem to a bounded visual question-answering task. The challenge is that many BoQ items require aggregating across multiple drawing locations (e.g., all external walls summed from multiple floor plan sheets).

### Pair Type B: Full Drawing Sheet + Section of BoQ
**Input**: A full floor plan image (possibly tiled into overlapping patches for high-res plans).
**Output**: A subset of BoQ line items corresponding to work visible on that sheet.

This matches closer to how a QS actually works but is harder to train: the model must learn spatial aggregation and the rules for what items a given drawing type can and cannot produce.

### Pair Type C: Multi-Modal Bundle + Full BoQ
**Input**: A set of drawing images (plan + section + elevation + schedule) for one building element or trade.
**Output**: All NRM2-compliant BoQ items for that element or trade section.

Most realistic to actual QS workflow. Hardest to label. Requires training examples that span multiple image inputs per output document. Closest to what commercial tools like Kreo and Civils.ai appear to be building internally.

### Pair Type D: Image + Structured Context + BoQ (hybrid VLM)
**Input**: Drawing image + text context (scale, drawing title, specification notes).
**Output**: BoQ JSON.

This is the most practical architecture: a vision-language model where the image encoder handles the drawing geometry and the language decoder handles description generation and rule application. The Florence-2 fine-tuning paper (arxiv 2411.03707) demonstrates this exact pattern for GD&T extraction from engineering drawings and achieved F1 improvements of 52% over GPT-4o zero-shot.

### What Ground Truth Looks Like
Each training example needs:
- The source drawing image(s), ideally as high-resolution raster (300+ DPI) or vector PDF
- The verified BoQ produced from those drawings by a qualified QS (MRICS or FRICS)
- Mapping between specific drawing regions and specific BoQ items (for attribution)
- The scale of the drawing (from title block or scale bar annotation)

---

## 6. Publicly Available Resources

### Standards Documents (Obtainable)
- NRM2 1st Edition PDF: directly linkable from rics.org (publicly accessible, copyright-protected)
- NRM2 October 2022 Update: same, publicly accessible
- SMM7 text: available academically (academia.edu has full uploads)
- NRM1: available from rics.org
- Laxton's NRM Building Price Book: commercially available, contains NRM2 work sections with rates

### Floor Plan and Drawing Datasets (Open)
- **CubiCasa5k**: 5,000 floor plan images, 80+ annotated categories (rooms, walls, doors, windows, icons). Finnish residential. Open under research license. Available on Kaggle and GitHub. Not dimensioned, but provides element segmentation ground truth.
- **FloorPlanCAD**: 15,000+ CAD floor plans in SVG format, residential to commercial. Project ended Jan 2023 but dataset accessible. Has vector primitives.
- **ArchCAD-400K** (March 2025): 5,538 complete architectural CAD drawings with 413,062 annotated chunks, 27 semantic categories. Largest publicly announced dataset. ArXiv: 2503.22346.
- **MLSTRUCT-FP**: Multi-unit floor plan dataset with wall polygons and slab labels in JSON. Available via public download request on GitHub.
- **MSD (ECCV 2024)**: First large-scale dataset of multi-apartment building complex floor plans. GitHub: caspervanengelenburg/msd.
- **ResPlan**: 17,000 residential floor plans, vector-graph format, permissive open-source license. ArXiv: 2508.14006.
- **Floor Plans 500** (Roboflow): 500 floor plan images with labelled doors, windows, functional zones.

### Bills of Quantities (Scarce)
This is the critical gap. There is no known public dataset of paired (drawing, BoQ) examples. What exists:
- Individual example BoQs attached to published tender notices on the UK government's Find a Tender service (gov.uk/find-tender) and Contracts Finder. These are real procurement documents but are unpaired from their source drawings.
- Institutional example BoQs from universities (GCU, UCL, Heriot-Watt publish QS course materials).
- Commercial templates on Scribd and similar platforms -- these are blank templates, not populated with actual quantities from real drawings.
- Academic papers in construction management journals sometimes include small BoQ excerpts.
- SRCC and similar institutions publish single-project example BoQs as procurement documents.

The paired dataset -- drawing image linked to specific BoQ items derived from it -- does not exist publicly. This is the single largest data gap.

### AI/ML Tools in This Space (Indicative of Feasibility)
- **Kreo**: UK-based AI takeoff platform, claims 95%+ precision on element identification. Internally trained, proprietary dataset.
- **Civils.ai**: AI for 2D CAD quantity takeoff, supports PDF and CAD input.
- **TaksoAi**: Patented computer vision for fitting/fixture/dimension extraction from blueprints.
- **Beam AI (iBeam)**: AI quantity surveying software marketed explicitly to QS professionals.
- **Quantity AI by Nested**: Supports PDF/JPEG/PNG blueprints, uses semantic segmentation for area extraction.

These companies' existence confirms the problem is tractable. Their proprietary datasets confirm the scarcity of open data.

---

## 7. Candidate Base Models

### Document Understanding Models (Strong Candidates)
- **Florence-2** (Microsoft, 0.23B parameters): Open-source VLM. The arxiv paper (2411.03707) demonstrates fine-tuning Florence-2 on 400 engineering drawings achieves +52% F1 and -43% hallucination rate vs. GPT-4o zero-shot on GD&T extraction. Directly applicable pattern.
- **Donut** (CLOVA AI, ECCV 2022): OCR-free document understanding transformer. Swin Transformer encoder + BART decoder. Fine-tuned on JSON-annotated document images. A May 2025 paper (arxiv 2505.01530) uses YOLO+Donut in two stages for engineering drawing parsing, achieving 97.3% F1. Very relevant architecture.
- **LayoutLMv3** (Microsoft): Integrates text, layout, and image modalities. Designed for document understanding tasks where spatial position of text matters. Strong for title block, schedule, and BoQ document parsing.
- **TrOCR** (Microsoft): Transformer-based OCR, state-of-the-art on printed text. Useful as a component for dimension annotation reading within a pipeline.

### Large Multimodal Models (Zero-Shot / Few-Shot)
- **GPT-4o**: Benchmarked on engineering drawings in the Florence-2 study. Capable zero-shot but high cost and hallucination rate (~43% worse than fine-tuned Florence-2 on this task).
- **Claude 3.5 Sonnet**: Also benchmarked in same study. Similar profile to GPT-4o for zero-shot drawing tasks.
- **Qwen2.5-VL**: Strong multimodal model with spatial reasoning. Relatively compact and fine-tunable.
- **InternVL2**: Open-source, strong document understanding benchmark scores.

### Architectural Drawing-Specific Models
- Research from CVPR 2021 (Lv et al., "Residential Floor Plan Recognition and Reconstruction") uses FCN + ResNet50 for line segment detection and YOLOv4 for number recognition. This remains a strong baseline architecture.
- DeepLabV3+ and Mask R-CNN have been applied to floor plan semantic segmentation.
- Vision Transformers (ViT) with global attention are well-suited to floor plan layout understanding where relationships between distant rooms matter.

### Recommended Base Architecture
A two-stage hybrid pipeline mirrors what current research shows works best:
1. **Stage 1 (Detection)**: YOLOv11 or similar object detector identifies regions of interest on the drawing (walls, openings, dimensions, room labels, scale bars).
2. **Stage 2 (Parsing)**: Florence-2 or Donut fine-tuned on those cropped regions produces structured JSON output (NRM2-compliant descriptions and quantities).

This matches the architecture from arxiv 2505.01530 and 2411.03707.

---

## 8. Key Technical Challenges

### Scale Detection and Calibration
Every measurement depends on correctly determining the drawing scale. Scale may be declared in the title block ("1:100"), shown as a scale bar, or both. Scale bars can be graphical (a line with tick marks and labels), which requires OCR plus geometric measurement. Plans are often printed at non-standard sizes, meaning nominal scale ("1:100") is unreliable without calibration from the graphical scale bar. This is a prerequisite challenge: without correct scale, every downstream quantity is wrong.

### Dimension Reading from Annotation
Architectural drawings use dimension chains: lines with arrowheads or ticks connecting to a numeric annotation. The model must:
1. Detect the dimension line geometry
2. Identify the termination points (what is being measured)
3. Read the numeric value via OCR
4. Associate the value with the correct drawing element

Text in drawings is often rotated 90 degrees, overlaps graphical elements, uses proprietary CAD fonts, and appears at inconsistent sizes. Standard OCR fails here. TrOCR and PaddleOCR perform better on rotated/oriented text but require domain-specific fine-tuning.

### Element Classification and Semantic Segmentation
A wall shown in a floor plan is a hatched rectangle. Whether it is:
- Loadbearing or non-loadbearing (different NRM2 items)
- Brick, blockwork, concrete, timber stud (different descriptions and rates)
- External or internal (different measurement rules)
- A cavity wall or solid (different items)

...requires the model to read hatch patterns, nearby text labels, specification cross-references, and sometimes draw inferences from structural drawings. This is the hardest classification problem because it is not purely visual -- it requires cross-document reasoning.

### Measurement Rule Application (NRM2 Compliance)
Even after correctly identifying and measuring an element, the model must apply the correct NRM2 measurement rules. For example:
- Voids in walls (openings for doors/windows) are deducted if over a threshold area (0.5m2 for some work sections). Small openings may be ignored. The model must know the deduction rules.
- Formwork is measured to contact faces only, not all faces.
- Reinforcement is deemed included in some concrete items; separate in others.
- Reveals, sills, and copings are measured separately under NRM2 but were deemed included under SMM7.

This requires encoding hundreds of rules as model knowledge -- either through fine-tuning on rule-adherent training examples, or through retrieval-augmented generation backed by the NRM2 text.

### Drawing Quality and Format Variation
Real drawings arrive as:
- High-res vector PDFs (extractable primitives, clean lines)
- Raster PDFs from scanned originals (lossy, noise, skew)
- Georeferenced CAD exports (DXF/DWG rendered to image)
- Photographs of paper drawings (worst case)

A robust model must handle all these. Vector PDFs allow layer-based extraction; raster images require full vision processing. This dramatically changes what preprocessing is possible.

### Aggregation Across Sheets
A real BoQ item (e.g., total external brickwork area) requires summing measurements from multiple floor plans (each floor), then subtracting opening areas shown on elevation drawings and door/window schedules. No single drawing contains all the information for most BoQ items. The model must maintain a measurement register and accumulate quantities across sheets -- a stateful process not natural to single-pass inference.

### Absence of Deduction Flags
A drawing shows a wall with a door opening. The model must:
1. Measure the gross wall area
2. Detect the door opening within the wall
3. Determine whether NRM2 requires deduction for that opening size
4. Apply the deduction correctly

This requires spatial reasoning (the opening is within the wall extents) plus rule lookup (deduction thresholds), simultaneously.

### Training Data Scarcity and Annotation Cost
The Florence-2 paper used 400 drawings. The ArchCAD-400K dataset uses 5,538 drawings. Neither is paired with BoQ outputs. Creating paired (drawing, BoQ) training data requires a qualified QS to verify every measurement -- at a professional billing rate. A realistic training set of 10,000 paired examples would require substantial professional QS time, likely hundreds of thousands of pounds in annotation cost.

### Measurement System Ambiguity
Some drawings do not clearly indicate whether they are metric or imperial. Older drawings or international projects may mix conventions. The model must infer or detect the unit system before any quantity is valid.

---

## Summary Assessment

| Dimension | Feasibility Rating | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Domain is well-defined | High | NRM2 provides explicit, codifiable rules |
| Base model candidates exist | High | Florence-2, Donut, LayoutLMv3 all applicable |
| Floor plan imagery datasets | Medium | Several open datasets exist; none paired to BoQ |
| BoQ training data (paired) | Low | No public paired dataset known; expensive to create |
| NRM2 rules as training signal | Medium | PDFs publicly accessible; need structured encoding |
| Scale detection | Medium-Hard | Active research problem; solvable with hybrid approach |
| Element classification | Hard | Requires multi-document cross-referencing |
| Rule application (NRM2) | Hard | 41 work sections, hundreds of deduction/inclusion rules |
| Commercial tools proving feasibility | Confirmed | Kreo, Civils.ai, Beam AI all exist and are funded |

### The Critical Bottleneck

The bottleneck is not the model architecture -- Florence-2 fine-tuning on paired (drawing region, structured output) pairs works demonstrably well. The bottleneck is **paired training data**: floor plan drawings matched to their verified NRM2-compliant BoQ items with attribution back to drawing regions. This data does not exist publicly and costs significant professional QS time to create. A realistic minimum viable dataset would be 2,000-5,000 drawing-region/BoQ-item pairs for a narrowed scope (e.g., one work section such as masonry or in-situ concrete), scaling up from there.

A practical path forward is a **narrowed-scope first phase**: target one NRM2 work section (e.g., Work Section 11: In-situ Concrete or Work Section 14: Masonry), build a curated annotated dataset of 500-1,000 paired examples with a QS collaborator, fine-tune Florence-2 or Donut, validate against RICS-published example drawings, and iterate outward.

---

tool_input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

## Project Overview

**British Quantity Surveyor (BQS)** -- AI vision model that reads architectural drawings and produces NRM2-compliant Bills of Quantities.

### Architecture
1. **Extraction Pipeline** (`extraction/`) -- Opens Revit models, exports sheet views as images, extracts element quantities per view via Revit API
2. **NRM2 Rules Engine** (`nrm2_rules/`) -- Maps Revit categories/parameters to NRM2 work sections with compliant descriptions
3. **Training Pipeline** (`training/`) -- Produces (image, BoQ JSON) training pairs, fine-tunes Florence-2 or Donut
4. **Scripts** (`scripts/`) -- Batch processing scripts for Revit model crawling

### Data Sources
- `//adam/datapool/projects/` -- 890 .rvt files (active projects 2019-2026)
- `//403Edge/Public/EBICArchive/` -- 293 .rvt files (archived projects)
- Total: 1,183 Revit models

### Training Pair Generation Strategy
For each Revit model with sheet views:
1. **Image**: Export sheet view as high-res PNG (what a QS would see)
2. **Label**: Query Revit API for all elements visible in that view -- walls (m2), doors (nr), windows (nr), concrete (m3), etc.
3. **Format**: Map Revit element data to NRM2-compliant BoQ line items (work section, description, unit, quantity)

### NRM2 Work Sections (Priority Order)
Phase 1: 14 (Masonry), 11 (In-situ Concrete), 22 (General Joinery), 23 (Windows), 24 (Doors)
Phase 2: 5 (Excavation), 15 (Structural Metalwork), 28-30 (Finishes)
Phase 3: 34-35 (MEP Services), remaining sections

### Key Standards
- **NRM2** (RICS New Rules of Measurement, 2nd edition) -- detailed measurement for building works
- **NRM1** -- order of cost estimating (early stage)
- 41 work sections with explicit measurement rules per element type

### Hardware
Caspar: i9-14900KF, 64GB DDR5, RTX 3090, 4TB NVMe

### Commands
```bash
# Extract training data from Revit models
python scripts/batch_extract.py --source "//adam/datapool/projects" --output output/

# Train vision model
python training/finetune.py --data output/training_pairs.json

# Run inference on a drawing image
python inference.py --image drawing.png
```

### NEVER/ALWAYS
**NEVER**: Commit .rvt files to the repo, hardcode network paths outside config
**ALWAYS**: Use NRM2 descriptions (not free-form), track extraction progress, validate quantities against model geometry

"""Map Revit element categories and parameters to NRM2 work section descriptions.

NRM2 (RICS New Rules of Measurement) defines 41 work sections with explicit
measurement rules. This module translates Revit API element data into
NRM2-compliant BoQ line items.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional


MM_TO_M = 0.001
FT_TO_M = 0.3048
FT2_TO_M2 = 0.09290304
FT3_TO_M3 = 0.028316846592


@dataclass
class BoQItem:
    """A single NRM2-compliant Bill of Quantities line item."""
    work_section: str       # e.g. "14.1.1"
    description: str        # NRM2-compliant description
    unit: str               # nr, m, m2, m3, kg, t
    quantity: float          # measured quantity
    element_ids: List[int]  # Revit element IDs that contribute to this item

    def to_dict(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        return {
            "work_section": self.work_section,
            "description": self.description,
            "unit": self.unit,
            "quantity": round(self.quantity, 2),
            "element_ids": self.element_ids,
        }


class NRM2Mapper:
    """Maps Revit elements to NRM2 BoQ items."""

    def map_wall(self, wall_data: Dict[str, Any]) -> Optional[BoQItem]:
        """Map a Revit wall element to NRM2 Section 14 (Masonry)."""
        area_ft2 = wall_data.get("area", 0)
        area_m2 = area_ft2 * FT2_TO_M2
        thickness_ft = wall_data.get("width", 0)
        thickness_mm = thickness_ft * FT_TO_M * 1000
        wall_type = wall_data.get("type_name", "Unknown")
        height_range = self._height_range(wall_data.get("height", 0) * FT_TO_M)

        description = (
            f"Brick/block walling; {wall_type}; "
            f"thickness {thickness_mm:.0f}mm; "
            f"height {height_range}"
        )

        return BoQItem(
            work_section="14.1.1",
            description=description,
            unit="m2",
            quantity=area_m2,
            element_ids=[wall_data.get("id", 0)],
        )

    def map_door(self, door_data: Dict[str, Any]) -> Optional[BoQItem]:
        """Map a Revit door element to NRM2 Section 24 (Doors)."""
        width_mm = door_data.get("width", 0) * FT_TO_M * 1000
        height_mm = door_data.get("height", 0) * FT_TO_M * 1000
        door_type = door_data.get("type_name", "Unknown")

        description = (
            f"Doors; {door_type}; "
            f"size {width_mm:.0f} x {height_mm:.0f}mm; "
            f"including ironmongery and frame"
        )

        return BoQItem(
            work_section="24.1.1",
            description=description,
            unit="nr",
            quantity=1,
            element_ids=[door_data.get("id", 0)],
        )

    def map_window(self, window_data: Dict[str, Any]) -> Optional[BoQItem]:
        """Map a Revit window element to NRM2 Section 23 (Windows)."""
        width_mm = window_data.get("width", 0) * FT_TO_M * 1000
        height_mm = window_data.get("height", 0) * FT_TO_M * 1000
        window_type = window_data.get("type_name", "Unknown")

        description = (
            f"Windows; {window_type}; "
            f"size {width_mm:.0f} x {height_mm:.0f}mm; "
            f"including glazing, frame, and ironmongery"
        )

        return BoQItem(
            work_section="23.1.1",
            description=description,
            unit="nr",
            quantity=1,
            element_ids=[window_data.get("id", 0)],
        )

    def map_floor(self, floor_data: Dict[str, Any]) -> Optional[BoQItem]:
        """Map a Revit floor element to NRM2 Section 11 (In-situ concrete) or 28 (Floor finishes)."""
        area_m2 = floor_data.get("area", 0) * FT2_TO_M2
        thickness_ft = floor_data.get("thickness", 0)
        thickness_mm = thickness_ft * FT_TO_M * 1000
        floor_type = floor_data.get("type_name", "Unknown")

        if "concrete" in floor_type.lower() or "slab" in floor_type.lower():
            volume_m3 = area_m2 * (thickness_mm / 1000)
            return BoQItem(
                work_section="11.1.1",
                description=f"In-situ concrete; {floor_type}; thickness {thickness_mm:.0f}mm",
                unit="m3",
                quantity=volume_m3,
                element_ids=[floor_data.get("id", 0)],
            )
        else:
            return BoQItem(
                work_section="28.1.1",
                description=f"Floor finishes; {floor_type}; to concrete base",
                unit="m2",
                quantity=area_m2,
                element_ids=[floor_data.get("id", 0)],
            )

    @staticmethod
    def _height_range(height_m: float) -> str:
        """NRM2 height ranges for walling."""
        if height_m <= 1.5:
            return "not exceeding 1.5m"
        elif height_m <= 3.0:
            return "1.5-3.0m"
        elif height_m <= 4.5:
            return "3.0-4.5m"
        elif height_m <= 6.0:
            return "4.5-6.0m"
        else:
            return f"exceeding 6.0m"

tool_input.content:
"""Extract training pairs from Revit models.

For each sheet view in a Revit model:
1. Export the view as a high-res PNG image
2. Query all visible elements and their quantities via Revit API
3. Map elements to NRM2 BoQ items
4. Produce a training pair: (image_path, boq_items)

This module defines the extraction logic. Actual Revit API calls
are made through the Revit add-in or Dynamo script (see scripts/).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Dict, List

from nrm2_rules.mapper import NRM2Mapper, BoQItem

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class TrainingPair:
    """A single (image, BoQ) training pair."""
    image_path: str
    model_path: str
    sheet_name: str
    view_name: str
    boq_items: List[Dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
    element_count: int = 0
    scale: str = ""

    def to_dict(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        return {
            "image_path": self.image_path,
            "model_path": self.model_path,
            "sheet_name": self.sheet_name,
            "view_name": self.view_name,
            "scale": self.scale,
            "element_count": self.element_count,
            "boq_items": self.boq_items,
        }


class RevitExtractor:
    """Processes Revit API output into training pairs."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.mapper = NRM2Mapper()

    def process_view_export(
        self,
        image_path: str,
        model_path: str,
        sheet_name: str,
        view_name: str,
        scale: str,
        elements: List[Dict[str, Any]],
    ) -> TrainingPair:
        """Convert raw Revit element data from a view into a training pair."""

        boq_items: List[BoQItem] = []

        for elem in elements:
            category = elem.get("category", "")
            item = None

            if category in ("Walls", "OST_Walls"):
                item = self.mapper.map_wall(elem)
            elif category in ("Doors", "OST_Doors"):
                item = self.mapper.map_door(elem)
            elif category in ("Windows", "OST_Windows"):
                item = self.mapper.map_window(elem)
            elif category in ("Floors", "OST_Floors"):
                item = self.mapper.map_floor(elem)

            if item is not None:
                boq_items.append(item)

        # Aggregate items by work section + description
        aggregated = self._aggregate_items(boq_items)

        pair = TrainingPair(
            image_path=image_path,
            model_path=model_path,
            sheet_name=sheet_name,
            view_name=view_name,
            scale=scale,
            element_count=len(elements),
            boq_items=[item.to_dict() for item in aggregated],
        )

        logger.info(
            "Extracted %d BoQ items from %d elements in %s / %s",
            len(aggregated), len(elements), sheet_name, view_name,
        )

        return pair

    @staticmethod
    def _aggregate_items(items: List[BoQItem]) -> List[BoQItem]:
        """Aggregate BoQ items with the same work section + description."""
        aggregated: Dict[str, BoQItem] = {}

        for item in items:
            key = f"{item.work_section}|{item.description}|{item.unit}"
            if key in aggregated:
                aggregated[key].quantity += item.quantity
                aggregated[key].element_ids.extend(item.element_ids)
            else:
                aggregated[key] = BoQItem(
                    work_section=item.work_section,
                    description=item.description,
                    unit=item.unit,
                    quantity=item.quantity,
                    element_ids=list(item.element_ids),
                )

        return sorted(aggregated.values(), key=lambda x: x.work_section)

    @staticmethod
    def save_training_pairs(pairs: List[TrainingPair], output_path: str) -> None:
        """Save training pairs to JSON."""
        path = Path(output_path)
        path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        data = [pair.to_dict() for pair in pairs]
        with open(path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            json.dump(data, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)
        logger.info("Saved %d training pairs to %s", len(data), path)

tool_input.content:
"""Dynamo Python Script: Extract sheet views and element data from open Revit model.

Run this inside a Dynamo Python Script node with the Revit model open.
It exports each sheet view as an image and dumps element quantities to JSON.

Inputs:
    IN[0]: Output directory path (string)

Outputs:
    OUT: List of exported file paths
"""

import clr
import json
import os
import sys

clr.AddReference('RevitAPI')
clr.AddReference('RevitAPIUI')
clr.AddReference('RevitServices')

from Autodesk.Revit.DB import *
from RevitServices.Persistence import DocumentManager

doc = DocumentManager.Instance.CurrentDBDocument
output_dir = IN[0] if isinstance(IN[0], str) else r"C:\temp\bqs_export"

os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)

FT_TO_M = 0.3048
FT2_TO_M2 = 0.09290304


def get_element_data(elem):
    """Extract key parameters from a Revit element."""
    data = {
        "id": elem.Id.IntegerValue,
        "category": elem.Category.Name if elem.Category else "Unknown",
        "type_name": elem.Name or "Unknown",
    }

    # Try to get common parameters
    for param_name in ["Area", "Volume", "Width", "Height", "Length", "Thickness"]:
        param = elem.LookupParameter(param_name)
        if param and param.HasValue:
            if param.StorageType == StorageType.Double:
                data[param_name.lower()] = param.AsDouble()
            elif param.StorageType == StorageType.Integer:
                data[param_name.lower()] = param.AsInteger()

    return data


def get_visible_elements(view):
    """Get all model elements visible in a view."""
    collector = FilteredElementCollector(doc, view.Id)
    collector.WhereElementIsNotElementType()

    target_categories = [
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Walls,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Doors,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Windows,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Floors,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_StructuralColumns,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_StructuralFoundation,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_StructuralFraming,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Roofs,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Ceilings,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Stairs,
    ]

    elements = []
    for cat in target_categories:
        cat_collector = FilteredElementCollector(doc, view.Id)
        cat_collector.OfCategory(cat)
        cat_collector.WhereElementIsNotElementType()
        for elem in cat_collector:
            try:
                elements.append(get_element_data(elem))
            except Exception:
                pass

    return elements


# Collect all sheet views
sheet_collector = FilteredElementCollector(doc).OfClass(ViewSheet)
results = []

for sheet in sheet_collector:
    sheet_name = sheet.SheetNumber + " - " + sheet.Name

    # Get views placed on this sheet
    viewport_ids = sheet.GetAllViewports()
    for vp_id in viewport_ids:
        viewport = doc.GetElement(vp_id)
        view_id = viewport.ViewId
        view = doc.GetElement(view_id)

        if view is None or view.ViewType == ViewType.Schedule:
            continue

        view_name = view.Name
        scale = "1:" + str(view.Scale) if hasattr(view, 'Scale') else "unknown"

        # Get visible elements in this view
        elements = get_visible_elements(view)

        if len(elements) == 0:
            continue

        # Save element data
        json_path = os.path.join(output_dir, f"{sheet.SheetNumber}_{view_name}.json")
        export_data = {
            "model_path": doc.PathName,
            "sheet_name": sheet_name,
            "view_name": view_name,
            "scale": scale,
            "elements": elements,
        }

        with open(json_path, "w") as f:
            json.dump(export_data, f, indent=2)

        results.append(json_path)

OUT = results

    collector = FilteredElementCollector(doc, view.Id)
    collector.WhereElementIsNotElementType()

    target_categories = [
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Walls,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Doors,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Windows,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Floors,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_StructuralColumns,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_StructuralFoundation,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_StructuralFraming,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Roofs,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Ceilings,
        BuiltInCategory.OST_Stairs,
    ]

    elements = []
    for cat in target_categories:
        cat_collector = FilteredElementCollector(doc, view.Id)
        cat_collector.OfCategory(cat)
        cat_collector.WhereElementIsNotElementType()
        for elem in cat_collector:
            try:
                elements.append(get_element_data(elem))
            except Exception:
                pass

    return elements


# Collect all sheet views
sheet_collector = FilteredElementCollector(doc).OfClass(ViewSheet)
results = []

for sheet in sheet_collector:
    sheet_name = sheet.SheetNumber + " - " + sheet.Name

    # Get views placed on this sheet
    viewport_ids = sheet.GetAllViewports()
    for vp_id in viewport_ids:
        viewport = doc.GetElement(vp_id)
        view_id = viewport.ViewId
        view = doc.GetElement(view_id)

        if view is None or view.ViewType == ViewType.Schedule:
            continue

        view_name = view.Name
        scale = "1:" + str(view.Scale) if hasattr(view, 'Scale') else "unknown"

        # Get visible elements in this view
        elements = get_visible_elements(view)

        if len(elements) == 0:
            continue

        # Save element data
        json_path = os.path.join(output_dir, f"{sheet.SheetNumber}_{view_name}.json")
        export_data = {
            "model_path": doc.PathName,
            "sheet_name": sheet_name,
            "view_name": view_name,
            "scale": scale,
            "elements": elements,
        }

        with open(json_path, "w") as f:
            json.dump(export_data, f, indent=2)

        results.append(json_path)

OUT = results

"""Batch extract training data from Revit models.

Scans network paths for .rvt files and orchestrates extraction
via the Revit MCP server or Dynamo automation.

Usage:
    python scripts/batch_extract.py --config config.yaml --output output/
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
import logging
from pathlib import Path
from typing import List

import yaml

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s")
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def discover_models(sources: List[dict]) -> List[Path]:
    """Scan source paths for .rvt files."""
    models = []
    for source in sources:
        path = Path(source["path"])
        if not path.exists():
            logger.warning("Source path not found: %s", path)
            continue
        rvt_files = sorted(path.rglob("*.rvt"))
        # Skip backup files (contain .0001., .0002., etc.)
        primary = [f for f in rvt_files if not any(
            f".{str(i).zfill(4)}." in f.name for i in range(1, 10000)
        )]
        logger.info("Found %d primary .rvt files in %s (%d total with backups)",
                     len(primary), source["name"], len(rvt_files))
        models.extend(primary)
    return models


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Batch extract BQS training data")
    parser.add_argument("--config", default="config.yaml")
    parser.add_argument("--output", default="output/")
    parser.add_argument("--dry-run", action="store_true", help="List models without extracting")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    with open(args.config) as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)

    models = discover_models(config["sources"])

    if args.dry_run:
        print(f"\nFound {len(models)} primary Revit models:\n")
        for m in models:
            print(f"  {m}")
        return

    # TODO: For each model, open via Revit MCP and run extraction
    # This requires the Revit MCP server to be running and a Revit instance available
    logger.info("Found %d models to process", len(models))
    logger.info("Extraction via Revit MCP not yet implemented -- use dry-run to list models")

    # Save model inventory
    inventory_path = Path(args.output) / "model_inventory.json"
    inventory_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    inventory = [{"path": str(m), "size_mb": m.stat().st_size / (1024 * 1024)} for m in models]
    with open(inventory_path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(inventory, f, indent=2)
    logger.info("Saved model inventory to %s", inventory_path)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
